metadata.title: Why Vague Revision Requests Change the Wrong Things
metadata.purpose: A vague revision request specifies outcome without specifying which elements to change and which to freeze. Claude treats the entire output as revision-eligible and applies the property globally — touching elements the user valued and never intended to modify.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: INK = named / frozen elements (preserved correctly); CRIMSON = unintended changes spreading from vague instruction
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO AI alignment research on instruction following, NO chain-of-thought or scratchpad techniques, NO model-comparison benchmarks on instruction compliance. Mechanism only: vague instruction = entire output revision-eligible.

beats.narration_text: This is Musinique. She asked Claude to make a press release more concise. Claude shortened it. It also removed the impact paragraph, flattened the CEO quote, and rewrote the opening hook. She had asked to change one thing. Four things changed.

card.copy: Why vague revision requests change the wrong things

beats.narration_text: This is Musinique. Maya is a communications director. Her press release is good — the opening hook lands, the CEO quote is strong, the impact data is on paragraph three. She types: make it more concise. The revision comes back shorter. Paragraph three is gone. The CEO quote is flattened to a single generic sentence. The hook is weaker.
beats.scene_description: Communications director at desk reading a revised press release printout with pen in hand, looking concerned at unexpected changes, editorial newsprint treatment, desaturated flat print collage
beats.image_prompt: B02, communications director woman at a desk, looking concerned while reading a printed press release with a pen in hand, editorial documentary newsprint treatment, desaturated flat print collage, professional office environment

beats.narration_text: Maya asked Claude to make the press release more concise. Nothing else. Claude changed four elements she never touched. Why does one vague revision request produce unintended changes throughout the output?

card.copy: Why does one revision request change four things?

beats.narration_text: Prompt design is the skill the platform doesn't want you to have, because a musician who can write a precise prompt is a musician who doesn't need to buy someone else's pre-packaged workflow. Every prompt in this video is a template you can adapt — paste it, swap the specifics, run it on your own catalog. The instruction is the tool.

artifactLines: The same tools the platforms use — pointed back at you.
Roll your own. Own the deck.

beats.narration_text: A revision request without named targets treats the entire output as revision-eligible. 'More concise' is a goal, not a target. Claude identifies every element that could plausibly serve that goal — and changes all of them. The result is a spread: one instruction, changes everywhere.

The output is not a set of independent elements. Changing one affects the others. Remove a paragraph for conciseness — the CEO quote no longer has a referent. Flatten the hook — the impact paragraph is now the lead. One unnamed change cascades into three.

What Claude needs is not just the change. It needs the wall around what must not be touched. A revision prompt has two parts: the target and the freeze list. Without the freeze list, every element is implicitly available.

Revision drift is not a model failure. It is a specification failure. The work order was incomplete. 'More concise' names the outcome but not the scope. Claude fills the gap with inference — and fills it across the whole output.

card.copy: Revision drift = specification failure, not model failure

beats.narration_text: The fix: name the target and name the freeze. 'Shorten the methodology section only. Do not touch the CEO quote, the impact paragraph, or the opening hook.' That revision request has a scope. Claude cannot spread to what is frozen.

This applies to any revision. 'Make it clearer' gives Claude the whole document. 'Make it warmer' gives Claude the whole document. The property you name becomes the optimization target across every sentence that could contribute to it.

A nonprofit director asks Claude to make a grant proposal 'more engaging.' Claude rewrites three paragraphs, adds a narrative hook, softens the data section, and removes the measurable outcomes list — because it read as dry. The funder required measurable outcomes. The section the director most needed to preserve is gone.

Targeted revision: 'Make the opening paragraph more engaging. Do not change the measurable outcomes section, the data in section two, or the logic model structure.' Claude rewrites the opening. The rest is untouched. The funder requirement survives.

The practice: before every revision request, name three things. The specific change target. The elements frozen — must not change. And ask for a change log after: what moved, what changed in language, what was kept exactly.

A vague revision gives Claude the whole document. Name the target. Freeze what must survive. Ask for a change log to confirm what moved. The revision loop only works if you own the scope. From Musinique.

card.copy: Name the target. Freeze the rest.
card.sub: The revision loop only works if you own the scope.
